ThinkPHP Day 8: What MVC Actually Means (and Why You Should Care)

Before we get into the later chapters, I have to pump the brakes for a second and say sorry — we need to understand the MVC (Model-View-Controller) pattern first. This chapter walks through what MVC actually is, because the concept makes everything we build later way easier to follow. We'll go through what each letter means, how the flow actually works, and why splitting your code this way saves you from future pain.
ThinkPHP Day 7: Where Does Everything Live? A Tour of the Directory Structure

We've already got ThinkPHP 5.1 installed, but instead of jumping straight into building stuff, let's slow down for a second. If you don't understand the directory structure, you'll have no idea where things are — or worse, where they're supposed to go. So in this chapter we'll walk through ThinkPHP's folder layout, look at the entry files, and even cover how to rename the core folder (and why I'd really rather you didn't).
ThinkPHP Day 6: Installing ThinkPHP with Composer

From this post on we finally get into the main event — the ThinkPHP chapters. But before we start actually writing ThinkPHP code, let's cover how to install ThinkPHP through Composer. And of course we'll tweak ThinkPHP a little along the way too.
Day5-Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch-MySQL Basics-Data Part 2 (SELECT Queries)

Last time I only covered INSERT, UPDATE, and DELETE, but I left out one thing on purpose: SELECT. The reason is that SELECT has so many possible combinations that it really deserves its own post. So in this one we'll dig into querying all columns, conditional queries with WHERE, AND/OR, operators, LIMIT, and sorting with ORDER BY. Let me show you why I split this out into a separate article.
Day 4 - Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch: MySQL Data Basics, Part 1 (CRUD)

Last time we learned the basics of working with tables. This time we'll dig into the data inside those tables — how to insert, read, update and delete rows. But before we touch any of that, there are a couple of concepts worth understanding first, because they'll make every action that comes later click into place.
Learning ThinkPHP from Scratch Day 3 - MySQL Tables: the SQL Commands You Actually Need

Last time we covered the basics of working with databases, but inside a database there's another thing called a table, and a table is where your data actually lives. You can picture it like a spreadsheet or a notebook. So this time we're going to learn the basic SQL commands for working with tables - creating them, inspecting columns, updating, deleting, adding columns, and wiping a table clean.
Learning ThinkPHP Day 2: SQL Basics — Working With Databases

Welcome to the first real post of the ThinkPHP series. Posts 1 through 4 cover the basics of SQL commands and operations, and once we're comfortable with those we'll jump into ThinkPHP proper. This time we start at the very bottom: creating, listing, and deleting databases in MySQL through phpMyAdmin. Let's get going!
Day 1 - Learning ThinkPHP From Scratch: An Introduction

ThinkPHP is a fairly uncommon framework, and I picked it as a challenge because I'd written with it before (and gotten beaten up by it). Back then I was on version 3.2.4, but the current version is 5.1, and ThinkPHP changed massively in between — even the system architecture is wildly different. So enough rambling, let's start learning ThinkPHP 5.1 from the ground up.

For anyone joining a team that uses Slack for the first time.
